perturbation.original_text: The Company shall pay Consultant a flat fee consulting rate of $18,000 per month.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company may pay Consultant a consulting fee, potentially around $18,000 per month, subject to internal budget considerations.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall pay' to 'may pay, potentially around' introduces ambiguity about the guaranteed payment, making it dependent on internal budget considerations. This undermines the certainty of compensation.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code § 201 requires prompt payment of wages to employees who are discharged or quit. While this agreement is for a consultant, misclassifying an employee as a consultant is a common issue. The modified text, making payment uncertain, would violate this code if the consultant is later determined to be misclassified as an employee under California law, as it does not guarantee prompt or full payment of earned wages.

EMPLOYMENT REGULATION AND SUPERVISION [200 - 2699.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) PART 1. COMPENSATION [200 - 452] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 1. Payment of Wages [200 - 273] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. General Occupations [200 - 244] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 201. (a) If an employer discharges an employee, the wages earned and unpaid at the time of discharge are due and payable immediately. An employer who lays off a group of employees by reason of the termination of seasonal employment in the curing, canning, or drying of any variety of perishable fruit, fish, or vegetables, shall be deemed to have made immediate payment when the wages of said employees are paid within a reasonable time as necessary for computation and payment thereof; provided, however, that the reasonable time shall not exceed 72 hours, and further provided that payment shall be made by mail to any employee who so requests and designates a mailing address therefor. (b) Notwithstanding any other law, the state employer shall be deemed to have made an immediate payment of wages under this section for any unused or accumulated vacation, annual leave, holiday leave, or time off to which the emp
perturbation.justification: The original text ensures a fixed monthly payment, while the changed text makes the payment uncertain and subject to 'internal budget considerations.' This undermines the employee's right to 'wages earned and unpaid at the time of discharge are due and payable immediately' as stated in California Labor Code § 201(a), provided in Scraped Law Snippet 1. By introducing ambiguity and potential non-payment, the modified contract contradicts the legal requirement for prompt and guaranteed wage payment upon discharge if the consultant is misclassified.

perturbation.original_text: Consultant agrees to indemnify and hold harmless the Company and its affiliates and subsidiaries and their respective directors, officers and employees from and against all taxes, losses, damages, liabilities, costs and expenses, including attorneys' fees and other legal expenses, arising directly or indirectly from or in connection with (i) any negligent, reckless or intentionally wrongful act of Consultant or Consultant's assistants, employees, contractors or agents, (ii) performance of the Services or any breach by the Consultant or Consultant's assistants, employees, contractors or agents of any of the covenants contained in this Agreement, (iii) any failure of Consultant to perform the Services in accordance with all applicable laws, rules and regulations, (iv) any violation or claimed violation of a third party's rights resulting in whole or in part from the Company's use of the Inventions or other deliverables of Consultant under this Agreement, or (v) any amounts Company is required to pay by any court or governmental authority in any country based on a finding that Consultant's employees or contractors engaged in the performance of the Services are employees of Company or the failure of Consultant to file documents with respect to such employees or contractors or to pay any tax or similar fee or assessment in any country.
perturbation.changed_text: Consultant will consider indemnifying the Company and its affiliates and subsidiaries and their respective directors, officers and employees from certain taxes, losses, damages, liabilities, costs and expenses, potentially including attorneys' fees and other legal expenses, arising from Consultant's actions, subject to a determination of responsibility and applicable insurance coverage.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Consultant agrees to indemnify and hold harmless' to 'Consultant will consider indemnifying' makes the indemnification obligation discretionary. Adding 'subject to a determination of responsibility and applicable insurance coverage' introduces further conditions that could significantly limit the scope of indemnification.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 2778 establishes rules for interpreting indemnity agreements. By making the indemnification discretionary and conditional, the modified text contradicts the intent of this section, which assumes a clearer and more defined indemnification obligation. The ambiguity introduced regarding 'determination of responsibility and applicable insurance coverage' creates a scenario where the indemnitee's (Company's) rights are less protected than intended by the statute.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 12. INDEMNITY [2772 - 2784.5] ( Title 12 enacted 1872. ) 2778. In the interpretation of a contract of indemnity, the following rules are to be applied, unless a contrary intention appears: 1. Upon an indemnity against liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is entitled to recover upon becoming liable; 2. Upon an indemnity against claims, or demands, or damages, or costs, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is not entitled to recover without payment thereof; 3. An indemnity against claims, or demands, or liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, embraces the costs of defense against such claims, demands, or liability incurred in good faith, and in the exercise of a reasonable discretion; 4. The person indemnifying is bound, on request of the person indemnified, to defend actions or proceedings brought against the latter in respect to the matters embraced by the indemnity, but the person indemnified has the right to conduct such defenses, if he chooses to do so; 5. If, after reques
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a definite obligation ('agrees to indemnify and hold harmless') with a discretionary one ('will consider indemnifying'). This introduces ambiguity regarding the consultant's responsibility. According to California Civil Code § 2778, indemnity contracts are interpreted with specific rules unless a contrary intention appears. The change undermines the clarity expected in indemnity agreements, creating a situation where the company's rights are less protected. The original text imposes a clear duty to indemnify, while the changed text introduces conditions ('determination of responsibility and applicable insurance coverage') that could significantly limit the scope of indemnification, contradicting the assumption of a clearer indemnification obligation.
